title: Xi Jinping opens WAIC 2026 as 29 nations sign the World AI Cooperation Organization charter
subtitle: China's president delivered his first in-person WAIC keynote and used it to launch a Shanghai-headquartered rival AI governance body — with founding members including Russia, Brazil, Indonesia, and 10 African states, and UN Secretary-General Guterres present at the signing.
author: AESOP AI Engine
body: President Xi Jinping delivered the opening keynote at the 2026 World AI Conference in Shanghai on July 17 — the first time he has attended the event in person since it launched in 2018. On the same day, twenty-nine nations formalized the World Artificial Intelligence Cooperation Organization, or WAICO, a new international body headquartered in Shanghai and structured under UN Charter principles. Chinese Foreign Minister Wang Yi signed alongside representatives from Russia, Belarus, Serbia, Cuba, Brazil, Venezuela, Pakistan, Indonesia, Kazakhstan, Laos, and roughly 10 African and 12 Asian states. UN Secretary-General António Guterres attended the ceremony.

Xi's speech gave WAICO its argument. He framed four observations on AI development — openness and win-win cooperation, security and controllability, people-centred deployment, and equity of access — and used the phrase 'AI should not be a solo performance by a single country, but a symphony of international cooperation.' He committed China to providing 5,000 training and seminar slots for developing countries over the next five years. That framing positions WAICO as an alternative to the OECD-adjacent, US-and-EU-anchored governance stack — one built around access for the Global South rather than compliance with frontier-lab safety commitments.

The absence in the room is as loud as the presence. No major U.S. AI firm signed on, and the U.S. has spent the last six months tightening rather than opening — Anthropic shut Chinese Claude access in early July, Alibaba banned Claude Code on its side of the wall, and the EU AI Office issued its Frontier Findings a day before WAIC opened. WAICO is now the second multilateral AI governance track alongside the UN's Global Dialogue that opened in Geneva in early July. Whether it becomes a real standards body or a diplomatic frame depends on what its members actually adopt in the next twelve months.

A note for learners: 'AI governance' will not be one system for much longer. If you are learning this space, watch for divergence — not just in rules but in what counts as evidence. A model release that satisfies WAICO's charter could be one a US-anchored framework rejects, and vice versa. The useful skill for a student or early-career engineer is being able to read a governance document and identify which failure modes it treats as central, which as marginal, and which as none of its business. That is what tells you what a governance regime will actually do.

sources.title: Xi Jinping calls for 'openness', opposes 'one country' rule in AI — South China Morning Post
